sourceQuestion:
Consider a machine with a byte addressable main memory of 2^32 bytes. Assume that a direct mapped data cache consisting of 32 lines of 64 bytes each is used in the system. A 50 x 50 two-dimensional array of bytes is stored in the main memory starting from memory location 1100H. Assume that the data cache is initially empty. The complete array is accessed twice. How many data cache misses will occur in total?
A. 48
B. 50
C. 56
D. 59

parsed.stem: Consider a machine with a byte addressable main memory of 2^32 bytes. Assume that a direct mapped data cache consisting of 32 lines of 64 bytes each is used in the system. A 50 x 50 two-dimensional array of bytes is stored in the main memory starting from memory location 1100H. Assume that the data cache is initially empty. The complete array is accessed twice. How many data cache misses will occur in total?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a machine with a byte addressable main memory of 2^32 bytes. Assume that a direct mapped data cache consisting of 32 lines of 64 bytes each is used in the system. A 50 x 50 two-dimensional array of bytes is stored in the main memory starting from memory location 1100H. Assume that the data cache is initially empty. The complete array is accessed twice. Which of the following lines of the data cache will be replaced by new blocks in accessing the array for the second time?
A. line 4 to line 11
B. line 4 to line 12
C. line 0 to line 7
D. line 0 to line 8

parsed.stem: Consider a machine with a byte addressable main memory of 2^32 bytes. Assume that a direct mapped data cache consisting of 32 lines of 64 bytes each is used in the system. A 50 x 50 two-dimensional array of bytes is stored in the main memory starting from memory location 1100H. Assume that the data cache is initially empty. The complete array is accessed twice. Which of the following lines of the data cache will be replaced by new blocks in accessing the array for the second time?

sourceQuestion:
For inclusion to hold between two cache levels L_1 and L_2 in a multi-level cache hierarchy, which of the following are necessary?
I. L_1 must be write-through cache
II. L_2 must be a write-through cache
III. The associativity of L_2 must be greater than that of L_1
IV. The L_2 cache must be at least as large as the L_1 cache
A. I only
B. I and IV only
C. II and IV only
D. I, II, and IV

parsed.stem:
For inclusion to hold between two cache levels L_1 and L_2 in a multi-level cache hierarchy, which of the following are necessary?
I. L_1 must be write-through cache
II. L_2 must be a write-through cache
III. The associativity of L_2 must be greater than that of L_1
IV. The L_2 cache must be at least as large as the L_1 cache

sourceQuestion:
Consider a 4-way set associative cache (initially empty) with total 16 cache blocks. The main memory consists of 256 blocks and the request for memory blocks are in the following order:
0, 255, 1, 4, 3, 8, 133, 159, 216, 129, 63, 8, 48, 32, 73, 92, 150.
Which one of the following memory block will NOT be in cache if LRU replacement policy is used?

A. 3
B. 8
C. 129
D. 216

parsed.stem:
Consider a 4-way set associative cache (initially empty) with total 16 cache blocks. The main memory consists of 256 blocks and the request for memory blocks are in the following order:
0, 255, 1, 4, 3, 8, 133, 159, 216, 129, 63, 8, 48, 32, 73, 92, 150.
Which one of the following memory block will NOT be in cache if LRU replacement policy is used?

sourceQuestion:
A computer system has an L1 cache, an L2 cache, and a main memory unit connected as shown below. The block size in L1 cache is 4 words. The block size in L2 cache is 16 words. The memory access times are 2 nanoseconds, 20 nanoseconds and 200 nanoseconds for L1 cache, L2 cache and the main memory unit respectively.

When there is a miss in L1 cache and a hit in L2 cache, a block is transferred from L2 cache to L1 cache. What is the time taken for this transfer?

A. 2 nanoseconds
B. 20 nanoseconds
C. 22 nanoseconds
D. 88 nanoseconds

parsed.stem:
A computer system has an L1 cache, an L2 cache, and a main memory unit connected as shown below. The block size in L1 cache is 4 words. The block size in L2 cache is 16 words. The memory access times are 2 nanoseconds, 20 nanoseconds and 200 nanoseconds for L1 cache, L2 cache and the main memory unit respectively.

When there is a miss in L1 cache and a hit in L2 cache, a block is transferred from L2 cache to L1 cache. What is the time taken for this transfer?

sourceQuestion:
A computer system has an L1 cache, an L2 cache, and a main memory unit connected as shown below. The block size in L1 cache is 4 words. The block size in L2 cache is 16 words. The memory access times are 2 nanoseconds, 20 nanoseconds and 200 nanoseconds for L1 cache, L2 cache and the main memory unit respectively.

When there is a miss in both L1 cache and L2 cache, first a block is transferred from the main memory to L2 cache, and then a block is transferred from L2 cache to L1 cache. What is the total time taken for the two transfers?

A. 222 nanoseconds
B. 888 nanoseconds
C. 902 nanoseconds
D. 968 nanoseconds

parsed.stem:
A computer system has an L1 cache, an L2 cache, and a main memory unit connected as shown below. The block size in L1 cache is 4 words. The block size in L2 cache is 16 words. The memory access times are 2 nanoseconds, 20 nanoseconds and 200 nanoseconds for L1 cache, L2 cache and the main memory unit respectively.

When there is a miss in both L1 cache and L2 cache, first a block is transferred from the main memory to L2 cache, and then a block is transferred from L2 cache to L1 cache. What is the total time taken for the two transfers?

sourceQuestion:
An 8 KB direct-mapped write-back cache is organized as multiple blocks, each of size 32-bytes. The processor generates 32-bit addresses. The cache controller contains the tag information for each cache block comprising of the following:
- 1 valid bit
- 1 modified bit
- As many bits as the minimum needed to identify the memory block mapped in the cache.

What is the total size of the cache tag directory in bits?

A. 4864 bits
B. 6144 bits
C. 4656 bits
D. 5376 bits

parsed.stem:
An 8 KB direct-mapped write-back cache is organized as multiple blocks, each of size 32-bytes. The processor generates 32-bit addresses. The cache controller contains the tag information for each cache block comprising of the following:
- 1 valid bit
- 1 modified bit
- As many bits as the minimum needed to identify the memory block mapped in the cache.

What is the total size of the cache tag directory in bits?

sourceQuestion:
A computer has a 256 KB, 4-way set associative, write back data cache with block size of 32 Bytes. The processor sends 32 bit addresses to the cache controller. Each cache tag directory entry contains, in addition to address tag, 2 valid bits, 1 modified bit and 1 replacement bit.

The number of bits in the tag field of an address is

A. 11
B. 14
C. 16
D. 27

The number of bits in the tag field of an address is

sourceQuestion:
A computer has a 256 KByte, 4-way set associative, write back data cache with block size of 32 Bytes. The processor sends 32 bit addresses to the cache controller. Each cache tag directory entry contains, in addition to address tag, 2 valid bits, 1 modified bit and 1 replacement bit. The size of the cache tag directory is:
A. 11 KBits
B. 14 KBits
C. 16 KBits
D. 27 KBits

sourceQuestion:
In a k-way set associative cache, the cache is divided into n/k sets, each of which consists of k lines. The lines of a set are placed in sequence one after another. The lines in set s+1 are sequenced before the lines in set s. The main memory blocks are numbered 0 onwards. The main memory block numbered j must be mapped to any of the cache lines from:
A. (floor(j/k) mod (n/k)) * k  to  (floor(j/k) mod (n/k)) * k + (k - 1)
B. (j mod n/k) * k  to  (j mod n/k) * k + (k - 1)
C. (j mod n/k)  to  (j mod n/k) + (k - 1)
D. (floor(j/n) mod (n/k)) * k  to  (floor(j/n) mod (n/k)) * k + (k - 1)
sourceAnswer: B. (j mod n/k) * k  to  (j mod n/k) * k + (k - 1)

parsed.stem: In a k-way set associative cache, the cache is divided into n/k sets, each of which consists of k lines. The lines of a set are placed in sequence one after another. The lines in set s+1 are sequenced before the lines in set s. The main memory blocks are numbered 0 onwards. The main memory block numbered j must be mapped to any of the cache lines from:

options.text: (j mod n/k) * k to (j mod n/k) * k + (k - 1)

sourceQuestion:
An access sequence of cache block addresses is of length N and contains n unique block addresses. The number of unique block addresses between two consecutive accesses to the same block address is bounded above by k. What is the miss ratio if the access sequence is passed through a cache of associativity >= k using LRU replacement?
A. n/N
B. 1/N
C. 1/n
D. k/n

parsed.stem: An access sequence of cache block addresses is of length N and contains n unique block addresses. The number of unique block addresses between two consecutive accesses to the same block address is bounded above by k. What is the miss ratio if the access sequence is passed through a cache of associativity >= k using LRU replacement?

sourceQuestion:
In designing a computer's cache system, the cache block (or cache line) size is an important parameter. Which one of the following statements is correct in this context?
A. A smaller block size implies better spatial locality
B. A smaller block size implies a smaller cache tag and hence lower cache tag overhead
C. A smaller block size implies a larger number of cache blocks and hence a potentially better cache hit rate due to spatial locality
D. A smaller block size implies a smaller miss penalty
sourceAnswer: D. A smaller block size implies a smaller miss penalty

parsed.stem: In designing a computer's cache system, the cache block (or cache line) size is an important parameter. Which one of the following statements is correct in this context?

options.text: A smaller block size implies better spatial locality
A smaller block size implies a smaller cache tag and hence lower cache tag overhead
A smaller block size implies a larger number of cache blocks and hence a potentially better cache hit rate due to spatial locality
A smaller block size implies a smaller miss penalty

sourceQuestion:
If the associativity of a processor cache is doubled while keeping the capacity and block size unchanged, which one of the following is guaranteed to be NOT affected?
A. Width of tag comparator
B. Width of set index decoder
C. Width of way-select multiplexer
D. Number of cache blocks

parsed.stem: If the associativity of a processor cache is doubled while keeping the capacity and block size unchanged, which one of the following is guaranteed to be NOT affected?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a machine with a byte addressable main memory of 2^20 bytes, block size of 16 bytes and a direct mapped cache having 512 cache lines. Let the addresses of two consecutive bytes in main memory be (E201F)_16 and (E2020)_16. What are the tag and cache line number (in hex) for main memory address (E2020)_16?
A. E, 202
B. F, 202
C. E, 20
D. 2, 01F

parsed.stem: Consider a machine with a byte addressable main memory of 2^20 bytes, block size of 16 bytes and a direct mapped cache having 512 cache lines. Let the addresses of two consecutive bytes in main memory be (E201F)_16 and (E2020)_16. What are the tag and cache line number (in hex) for main memory address (E2020)_16?

sourceQuestion:
A file system uses an in-memory cache to cache disk blocks. The miss rate of the cache is shown in the figure (miss rate decreases as cache size increases from 0 to 100 MB). The latency to read a block from the cache is 1 ms and to read a block from the disk is 10 ms. Assume that the cost of checking whether a block exists in the cache is negligible. Available cache sizes are in multiples of 10 MB. The smallest cache size required to ensure that the average read access time is less than 6 ms is _______ MB.
A. 10
B. 20
C. 30
D. 40

parsed.stem: A file system uses an in-memory cache to cache disk blocks. The miss rate of the cache is shown in the figure (miss rate decreases as cache size increases from 0 to 100 MB). The latency to read a block from the cache is 1 ms and to read a block from the disk is 10 ms. Assume that the cost of checking whether a block exists in the cache is negligible. Available cache sizes are in multiples of 10 MB. The smallest cache size required to ensure that the average read access time is less than 6 ms is _______ MB.

sourceQuestion:
In a two-level cache system, the access times of L1 and L2 caches are 1 and 8 clock cycles, respectively. The miss penalty from the L2 cache to main memory is 18 clock cycles. The miss rate of L2 cache is twice that of L1. The average memory access time (AMAT) of this cache system is 4.5 cycles. The miss rates of L1 and L2 respectively are:
A. 0.111 and 0.056
B. 0.056 and 0.111
C. 0.0892 and 0.1784
D. 0.1784 and 0.0892

parsed.stem: In a two-level cache system, the access times of L1 and L2 caches are 1 and 8 clock cycles, respectively. The miss penalty from the L2 cache to main memory is 18 clock cycles. The miss rate of L2 cache is twice that of L1. The average memory access time (AMAT) of this cache system is 4.5 cycles. The miss rates of L1 and L2 respectively are:

sourceQuestion:
The read access times and the hit ratios for different caches in a memory hierarchy are as given below:

Cache    Read access time (in nanoseconds)    Hit ratio
l-cache        2                                  0.8
d-cache        4                                  0.9
L2-cache       8                                  0.9

The read access time of main memory is 90 nanoseconds. Assume that the caches use a referred-word-first read policy and the writeback policy. Assume that the dirty bit is always 0 (clean). The average read access time in nanoseconds (up to 2 decimal places) is ________.
A. 4.72
B. 5.46
C. 6.0
D. 7.0

parsed.stem:
The read access times and the hit ratios for different caches in a memory hierarchy are as given below:

Cache    Read access time (in nanoseconds)    Hit ratio
l-cache        2                                  0.8
d-cache        4                                  0.9
L2-cache       8                                  0.9

The read access time of main memory is 90 nanoseconds. Assume that the caches use a referred-word-first read policy and the writeback policy. Assume that the dirty bit is always 0 (clean). The average read access time in nanoseconds (up to 2 decimal places) is ________.

sourceQuestion:
The size of the physical address space of a processor is 2^N bytes. The word length is 2^2 bytes. The capacity of cache memory is 2^M bytes. The size of each cache block is 2^4 words. For a K-way set-associative cache memory, the length (in number of bits) of the tag field is
A. N - M - 2
B. N - M + log_2(K)
C. N - M - 4 + log_2(K)
D. N - M - 2 + log_2(K)

parsed.stem: The size of the physical address space of a processor is 2^N bytes. The word length is 2^2 bytes. The capacity of cache memory is 2^M bytes. The size of each cache block is 2^4 words. For a K-way set-associative cache memory, the length (in number of bits) of the tag field is

sourceQuestion:
A certain processor uses a fully associative cache of size 16 kB. The cache block size is 16 bytes. Assume that the main memory is byte addressable and uses a 32-bit address. How many bits are required for the Tag and the Index fields respectively in the addresses generated by the processor?
A. 24 bits and 0 bits
B. 24 bits and 8 bits
C. 28 bits and 0 bits
D. 28 bits and 4 bits

parsed.stem: A certain processor uses a fully associative cache of size 16 kB. The cache block size is 16 bytes. Assume that the main memory is byte addressable and uses a 32-bit address. How many bits are required for the Tag and the Index fields respectively in the addresses generated by the processor?

sourceQuestion:
A computer system with a word length of 32 bits has a 16 MB byte-addressable main memory and a 64 KB, 4-way set associative cache memory with a block size of 256 bytes. Consider the following four physical addresses represented in hexadecimal notation.
A1 = 0x42A4,  A2 = 0xA4BB,  A3 = 0x6A39C,  A4 = 0x5E4B80
Which one of the following is TRUE?
A. A1 and A4 are mapped to different cache sets.
B. A2 and A3 are mapped to the same cache set.
C. A3 and A4 are mapped to the same cache set.
D. A1 and A3 are mapped to the same cache set.
sourceAnswer: A. A1 and A4 are mapped to different cache sets.

parsed.stem:
A computer system with a word length of 32 bits has a 16 MB byte-addressable main memory and a 64 KB, 4-way set associative cache memory with a block size of 256 bytes. Consider the following four physical addresses represented in hexadecimal notation.
A1 = 0x42A4,  A2 = 0xA4BB,  A3 = 0x6A39C,  A4 = 0x5E4B80
Which one of the following is TRUE?

options.text: A1 and A4 are mapped to different cache sets.
A2 and A3 are mapped to the same cache set.
A3 and A4 are mapped to the same cache set.
A1 and A3 are mapped to the same cache set.

sourceQuestion:
Assume a two-level inclusive cache hierarchy, L1 and L2, where L2 is the larger of the two. Consider the following statements.
S1: Read misses in a write-through L1 cache do not result in writebacks of dirty lines to the L2 cache.
S2: Write allocate policy must be used in conjunction with write-through caches and no-write allocate policy must be used with writeback caches.
Which of the following statements is correct?
A. S1 is true and S2 is false.
B. S1 is false and S2 is true.
C. Both S1 and S2 are true.
D. Both S1 and S2 are false.
sourceAnswer: A. S1 is true and S2 is false.

parsed.stem:
Assume a two-level inclusive cache hierarchy, L1 and L2, where L2 is the larger of the two. Consider the following statements.
S1: Read misses in a write-through L1 cache do not result in writebacks of dirty lines to the L2 cache.
S2: Write allocate policy must be used in conjunction with write-through caches and no-write allocate policy must be used with writeback caches.
Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: S1 is true and S2 is false.
S1 is false and S2 is true.
Both S1 and S2 are true.
Both S1 and S2 are false.

sourceQuestion:
Let WB and WT be two set associative cache organizations that use LRU (Least Recently Used) algorithm for cache block replacement. WB is a write back cache and WT is a write through cache. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

A. Each cache block in WB and WT has a dirty bit.
B. Every write hit in WT leads to a data transfer from cache to main memory.
C. Eviction of a block from WT will not lead to data transfer from cache to main memory.
D. A read miss in WB will never lead to eviction of a dirty block from WB.

parsed.stem: Let WB and WT be two set associative cache organizations that use LRU (Least Recently Used) algorithm for cache block replacement. WB is a write back cache and WT is a write through cache. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

options.text: Each cache block in WB and WT has a dirty bit.
Every write hit in WT leads to a data transfer from cache to main memory.
Eviction of a block from WT will not lead to data transfer from cache to main memory.
A read miss in WB will never lead to eviction of a dirty block from WB.

sourceQuestion:
Consider a system with direct mapped data cache with a block size of 64 bytes. The system has a physical address space of 4 KB and a word length of 16 bits. During the execution of a program, four data words P, Q, R, and S are accessed in that order 10 times. Hence, there are 40 accesses to data cache altogether. Assume that the data cache is initially empty. The addresses of the first bytes of P, Q, R, and S are 0xA248, 0xC28A, 0xCA8A, and 0xC282, respectively. Assume that during the execution of the above program, only data words P, Q, R, S are being accessed.

Which of the following statements is/are TRUE with respect to the data cache?

A. 6 * 2^0 = 6
B. Once P is brought to the cache, it is never evicted.
C. At the end of execution only R and S reside in the cache.
D. Every access to Q evicts P from the cache.
sourceAnswer: C. At the end of execution only R and S reside in the cache.

parsed.stem:
Consider a system with direct mapped data cache with a block size of 64 bytes. The system has a physical address space of 4 KB and a word length of 16 bits. During the execution of a program, four data words P, Q, R, and S are accessed in that order 10 times. Hence, there are 40 accesses to data cache altogether. Assume that the data cache is initially empty. The addresses of the first bytes of P, Q, R, and S are 0xA248, 0xC28A, 0xCA8A, and 0xC282, respectively. Assume that during the execution of the above program, only data words P, Q, R, S are being accessed.

Which of the following statements is/are TRUE with respect to the data cache?

options.text: Once P is brought to the cache, it is never evicted.
At the end of execution only R and S reside in the cache.
Every access to Q evicts P from the cache.

sourceQuestion:
Consider two set-associative cache memory architectures: WBC, which uses the write back policy, and WTC, which uses the write through policy. Both of them use the LRU (Least Recently Used) block replacement policy. The cache memory is connected to the main memory. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

A. A read miss in WBC never evicts a dirty block.
B. A read miss in WBC triggers a write back operation of a cache block to main memory.
C. A write hit in WTC can modify the value of the dirty bit of a cache block.
D. A write miss in WTC always writes the victim cache block to main memory before loading the missed block to the cache.
sourceAnswer: B. A read miss in WBC triggers a write back operation of a cache block to main memory.

parsed.stem: Consider two set-associative cache memory architectures: WBC, which uses the write back policy, and WTC, which uses the write through policy. Both of them use the LRU (Least Recently Used) block replacement policy. The cache memory is connected to the main memory. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

options.text: A read miss in WBC never evicts a dirty block.
A read miss in WBC triggers a write back operation of a cache block to main memory.
A write hit in WTC can modify the value of the dirty bit of a cache block.
A write miss in WTC always writes the victim cache block to main memory before loading the missed block to the cache.

sourceQuestion:
Consider a memory system with 1G bytes of main memory and 16K bytes of cache memory. Assume that the processor generates 20-bit memory address, and the cache block size is 16 bytes. If the cache uses direct mapping, how many bits will be required to store all the tag values? [Assume memory is byte addressable, 1K = 2^10, 1M = 2^20.]

A. 6 * 2^7
B. 8 * 2^7
C. 6 * 2^7
D. 2^7

parsed.stem: Consider a memory system with 1G bytes of main memory and 16K bytes of cache memory. Assume that the processor generates 20-bit memory address, and the cache block size is 16 bytes. If the cache uses direct mapping, how many bits will be required to store all the tag values? [Assume memory is byte addressable, 1K = 2^10, 1M = 2^20.]

sourceQuestion:
For a direct-mapped cache, 5 bits are used for the tag field and 3 bits are used to index into a cache block. The size of each cache block is one byte. Assume that there is no other information stored for each cache block. Which ONE of the following is the CORRECT option for the sizes of the main memory and the cache memory in this system (byte addressable)?

A. 64 KB and 8 B
B. 128 KB and 16 B
C. 64 KB and 8 KB
D. 128 KB and 8 KB and 6 KB

parsed.stem: For a direct-mapped cache, 5 bits are used for the tag field and 3 bits are used to index into a cache block. The size of each cache block is one byte. Assume that there is no other information stored for each cache block. Which ONE of the following is the CORRECT option for the sizes of the main memory and the cache memory in this system (byte addressable)?

sourceQuestion:
Consider a system with 2-level cache. Access times of Level 1 cache, Level 2 cache and main memory are 1 ns, 10 ns, and 500 ns respectively. The hit rates of Level 1 and Level 2 caches are 0.9 and 0.8, respectively. What is the average access time of the system ignoring the search time within the cache?

A. 13.0
B. 12.8
C. 12.0
D. 12.4

parsed.stem: Consider a system with 2-level cache. Access times of Level 1 cache, Level 2 cache and main memory are 1 ns, 10 ns, and 500 ns respectively. The hit rates of Level 1 and Level 2 caches are 0.9 and 0.8, respectively. What is the average access time of the system ignoring the search time within the cache?
